nodes.name: When Executed by WF-001
nodes.notes: Accept all data (passthrough): the caller's full payload — including any binary — crosses the sub-workflow boundary unchanged. inputSource must be the lowercase 'passthrough'; camelCase 'passThrough' is silently ignored on import and the trigger falls back to defined-fields mode.

nodes.name: Load PR-001 + LLM Settings

parameters.query: SELECT (SELECT settings FROM configuration.providers WHERE kind='llm') AS settings, system_prompt, user_prompt FROM configuration.prompt_versions WHERE prompt_id='PR-001' ORDER BY version DESC LIMIT 1;

nodes.notes: Loads prompt body (R-08/§608, never embedded) + llm settings. CRIE Postgres credential.

nodes.name: plan() — Batch Plan
nodes.notes: Derives BOTH budgets from TPM and measured paragraph size: a batch of N paragraphs costs N*promptPerPara + N*completionPerPara, and TPM caps the SUM. Setting the two independently either wastes the input window or overruns the output ceiling (finish_reason='length'). Solves for the split where both are exhausted simultaneously, then sets max_tokens = completionTarget * safetyFactor so a density underestimate still does not truncate. Planner block is pure and lift-ready. MEMORY: drops ocr.raw (11.4MB of Azure geometry that nothing reads) and stores batches as index RANGES rather than paragraph copies. Item 15MB -> ~1.4MB; retained execution heap ~2.7GB -> ~250MB.

nodes.name: more batches?
nodes.notes: true -> execute the next batch. false -> every batch done; proceed to merge().

nodes.name: build-request() — Render Batch
nodes.notes: Renders ONE batch. PR-001 is unchanged — its {{ocr}} placeholder receives a batch excerpt instead of the whole document. The heading breadcrumb is fenced as reference context so the model does not extract from it. maxTokens = reservedOutputTokens (the budget the planner reserved). Emits _batchMarker so collect() can verify the state it restores by node reference matches the batch actually sent (the HTTP node drops the item — §3.5). Rehydrates the batch's paragraphs from ocr.paragraphs by index (batches are stored as ranges to keep the looping item small).

nodes.name: OpenAI Chat Completions

parameters.jsonBody: ={{ JSON.stringify({ model: $json._model, temperature: $json._temp, max_tokens: $json._max, response_format: { type: 'json_object' }, messages: $json.messages }) }}

nodes.notes: timeout 300s: generating requestMaxTokens (~8k) at ~30-60 tok/s can take minutes; the previous 45s timeout would abort long batches mid-generation and surface as a network error rather than a completion-budget problem. HTTP-node retries are DISABLED on purpose: collect() owns retry/backoff (429/5xx -> exponential backoff, retry the SAME batch). A second retry layer here would re-send the full prompt+completion, burning TPM invisibly to the pacing logic. neverError+fullResponse so collect() sees statusCode and can decide.

nodes.name: collect() — Validate + Accumulate
nodes.notes: Restores loop state by NODE REFERENCE from build-request() — the OpenAI HTTP node REPLACES the item and drops _bx (§3.5). Verifies _batchMarker so a mis-resolved reference fails loudly instead of attributing a response to the wrong batch. Rebuilds the item from the trigger. Validates locators, accumulates results, advances the cursor. 429/5xx -> backoff, retry the SAME batch. Pacing from actual usage.total_tokens, capped at 55s, 0 after the final batch. Logs response length/finish_reason/tail BEFORE parsing. Throws a specific error on finish_reason='length' (output-budget exhaustion — the JSON is TRUNCATED, not malformed) instead of a misleading parse error. Detects density drift by comparing actual completion tokens against the planner's budget.

nodes.name: pace() — TPM Throttle
nodes.notes: Throttles to keep the rolling 60s token spend under tpmBudget. Duration is set by collect() from ACTUAL token usage, capped at 55s to stay under n8n's ~65s DB-offload threshold (unsupported with Queue Mode + filesystem binary), and 0 after the final batch.

nodes.name: merge() — Dedup + Rank
nodes.notes: Merges all batch outputs. Dedup on normalized statement; when duplicates exist the occurrence with the strongest EVIDENCE QUALITY wins (authoritySource > specific category > sourcePage > body text > lowest paragraph as deterministic tie-break) — NOT first-by-document-order, which would prefer a vague exec-summary paraphrase over the normative statement. Conflicts are logged for observability only. Emits { knowledge: { knowledgeUnits } } — the exact contract SW-009 already consumes. No schema change. Emits the FULL original ocr (spreads the trigger payload); the lean ocr built by plan() is confined to the loop item and never leaves SW-008. Guarded by an assertion.

meta.note: LLM via HTTP Request node; prompt loaded from configuration.prompt_versions (never embedded).
meta.enrichment: output = {...incomingItem, newResult}; never discards accumulated state
meta.jsonbFix: settings is jsonb (object); removed JSON.parse on settings; LLM-content parse retained
meta.triggerFix: executeWorkflowTrigger upgraded to 1.1 inputSource=passThrough for binary passthrough
changelog: v1.4.1: Fixed stale copy-paste reference. 'Build LLM Request' referenced $('Load PR-002 + LLM Settings') — a node that does not exist in SW-008 (its node is 'Load PR-001 + LLM Settings'). Would have thrown "hasn't been executed" at runtime. Reference retargeted; stale PR-002 comment corrected. No logic change.

v1.5.0: Build LLM Request no longer serializes the whole ocr object (which embeds ocr.raw = the full Azure analyzeResult). It now substitutes flattened page/paragraph-marked plain text, cutting the prompt ~25x and keeping it inside the model context. Page/paragraph markers preserved for PR-001 sourcePage/sourceParagraph. Adds maxPromptChars budget.

v1.6.0: flattenOcr prefers provider paragraphs (Azure analyzeResult.paragraphs via ocr.pages[].paragraphs), falling back to blank-line splitting only when absent. Emits document statistics {ocrPages, ocrCharacters, approxPromptTokens, truncated, paragraphSource} to the execution log and onto _docStats.

v2.0.0: consumes the normalized OCR model. flattenOcr walks ocr.paragraphs[] (universal unit) instead of ocr.pages[].text (which collapsed DOCX to one page and discarded the document). Locators are [pN:paraM] for paginated formats and [paraM] for DOCX. Stats now include ocrParagraphs, sourceFormat and pagination.

v3.0.0: BATCHED EXTRACTION. Replaces the single truncated LLM call (maxPromptChars=360000, which silently discarded ~2/3 of large documents) with a token-budgeted batch loop: plan() -> [more batches? -> build-request() -> OpenAI -> collect() -> pace()] -> merge(). Batch results ACCUMULATE ON THE ITEM (Guidelines §3.6 — run history is not reconstructible downstream). Per-batch locator validation rejects hallucinated sourceParagraph values. Merge dedups on normalized statement and ranks by EVIDENCE QUALITY, not document order. Conflicts logged (observability only). PR-001 UNCHANGED. Emits { knowledge: { knowledgeUnits } } — the exact contract SW-009 consumes. NO schema change, NO downstream change, NO WF-001 change.

v3.0.0 (pacing correction): pacing is derived from ACTUAL usage.total_tokens in collect(), not from an a-priori per-batch estimate in plan(). The estimate produced a fixed ~46s wait per batch — close to n8n's ~65s Wait threshold above which execution is offloaded to the DB (unsupported under Queue Mode + filesystem binary) — and it also waited after the final batch for no reason. Now: wait proportional to tokens actually spent, HARD-CAPPED at 55s, and skipped entirely after the last batch.

v3.0.1: FIX — collect() read $json._bx, but the OpenAI HTTP Request node REPLACES the item and drops it (Engineering Guidelines §3.5, which this code violated). The item reaching collect() is only the API response, so _bx was undefined and st.batches[st.cursor] threw. collect() now restores loop state by node reference from build-request() (the last node before the HTTP call that carries it) and rebuilds the item from the trigger. build-request() stamps _batchMarker so collect() verifies it restored state for the batch actually sent. Reading the CURRENT run of an upstream node is reliable; this is distinct from reconstructing EARLIER loop runs, which §3.6 establishes as unreliable.

v3.1.0: DUAL-BUDGET PLANNER + truncation detection. (a) The planner budgeted only PROMPT tokens and assumed output would fit a fixed reserve; a batch could fit the input window while overflowing the output ceiling -> finish_reason='length' -> valid-but-TRUNCATED JSON, misreported as 'unparseable JSON'. plan() now solves BOTH budgets together from TPM: a batch of N paragraphs costs N*promptPerPara + N*completionPerPara and TPM caps the SUM, so it packs to the point where both are exhausted at once. max_tokens = completionTarget x completionSafetyFactor (default 2.0), capped at maxOutputTokens. (b) collect() logs response length/finish_reason/tail before parsing, throws a SPECIFIC error on finish_reason='length', and warns on density drift (actual completion >> budgeted). NOTE: the safety factor is applied INSIDE the budget solve — max_tokens is what the provider RESERVES against TPM, so inflating it after solving would exceed the per-minute budget and cause 429s. requestMaxTokens is additionally clamped to the TPM headroom left after the prompt.

v3.1.1: OpenAI node options corrected for the larger max_tokens. (a) timeout 45s -> 300s: requestMaxTokens is now ~8,344 (up from a fixed 4,096), and generating that many tokens can take minutes; a 45s timeout would abort mid-generation and surface as a network error, masking the real cause. (b) HTTP-node maxRetries removed: collect() already owns 429/5xx backoff and retries the same batch. A second retry layer re-sends the whole prompt+completion, burning TPM invisibly to the pacing logic. (c) fullResponse+neverError so collect() sees statusCode and finish_reason rather than n8n throwing first.

v3.1.1 (density telemetry): collect() records the OBSERVED extraction density per batch and merge() reports the measured document density against the planned one, with a tuning hint. plan() remains STATIC (it runs once, upstream of the loop) — this is telemetry, not adaptation. It provides the evidence needed to tune extractionDensity per corpus, and to decide whether adaptive re-planning is worth building. See CRIE_Adaptive_Density_Design.md.

v3.2.0: MEMORY. The looping item was ~15 MB and n8n retains every node's output for every loop iteration: 15MB x 4 retaining nodes x 18 iterations x ~2.5 object overhead ~= 2.7 GB, past Node's default ~2 GB heap cap (host RAM is irrelevant to the V8 cap). Root cause: (1) ocr.raw is 11.4 MB of Azure geometry that NOTHING in the pipeline reads — verified across WF-001 and every SW — yet it rode every iteration; (2) _bx.batches stored COPIES of all 10,729 paragraph objects. Now: plan() drops ocr.raw (and content/tables/figures, also unread in the loop) and stores batches as index RANGES; build-request() rehydrates paragraphs from ocr.paragraphs. Item ~15MB -> ~1.4MB; retained heap ~2.7GB -> ~250MB. No contract change; no Object Storage needed.

v3.2.1: makes the SCOPE of the v3.2.0 memory optimisation explicit and enforced. The lean ocr (no raw/content/tables/figures) exists ONLY on the loop item; merge() returns the FULL original ocr to WF-001 by spreading the trigger payload. Previously correct but incidental — now asserted, so a later edit cannot silently start emitting the lean copy. Audit: no consumer (WF-001 node-ocr-validate / node-knowledge / node-chunk / node-cert, SW-005, SW-007, SW-013, SW-014) reads raw/content/tables/figures; and the OCR result is never persisted (no Postgres node writes it, no migration stores it).

v3.3.0: (1) Execute Workflow Trigger set to Accept all data — inputSource 'passthrough' (lowercase), typeVersion 1.1. (2) PLANNER CALIBRATION from the live batch-2 failure: planned 669 paragraphs / 4,416 estimated completion tokens; actual completion 8,832 = max_tokens, i.e. a CENSORED observation whose true demand was higher. Measured density >= 8832/(669x55) = 0.240 KU/para, at least 2.0x the 0.12 default. extractionDensity default raised 0.12 -> 0.30 (25% above the observed floor, because the measurement is a lower bound and an SRS's normative sections exceed its mean). Effect: ~426 paragraphs/batch instead of 669 (-36%), and at the expected density the model sits at ~50% of its output ceiling rather than at it; the plan survives true density up to ~0.60 KU/para. Deliberately NOT fixed by raising completionSafetyFactor: the safety factor absorbs VARIANCE around a correct estimate, and using it to correct systematic BIAS would leave completionTarget, the drift detector and the density telemetry all wrong while keeping batches at their ceiling.
